families.name: Against the person
families.prompt: Does it go after the person, instead of their actual point?

families.name: Leans on something beside the evidence
families.prompt: Does it lean on a feeling, a name, or popularity instead of reasons?

families.name: Leaps past the evidence
families.prompt: Does the conclusion reach further than the evidence really supports?

families.name: Assumes what it should establish
families.prompt: Does it treat its starting point as true without proving it?

families.name: Changes the subject
families.prompt: Does it change the subject instead of answering the point?

families.name: Plays on feelings
families.prompt: Does it work on your feelings instead of giving reasons?

families.name: Leans on who or how many
families.prompt: Does it rest on who said it, or how many agree, instead of evidence?

families.name: Forces a narrow choice
families.prompt: Does it act like there are only two options when there aren’t?

families.name: Assumes what it's proving
families.prompt: Does its support just repeat the claim it’s trying to prove?

families.name: Shifts the burden of proof
families.prompt: Does it say something’s true just because no one proved it false?

families.name: Jumps from too little
families.prompt: Does it jump to a big conclusion from too few examples?

families.name: Mistakes the cause
families.prompt: Does it assume one thing caused another just because they’re linked?

families.name: Compares unlike things
families.prompt: Does it compare two things that aren’t really alike?

families.name: Slides between meanings
families.prompt: Does a key word quietly switch meaning partway through?

families.name: Confuses parts and wholes
families.prompt: Does it assume the parts and the whole must share a trait?

families.name: If-then mix-up
families.prompt: Does it draw the wrong move from an “if this, then that” step?

families.name: Misreads the numbers
families.prompt: Does it draw a shaky conclusion from numbers or chance?

families.name: Other ways it dodges
families.prompt: Does it answer something close to the point, but not the point itself?

families.name: Other hidden assumptions
families.prompt: Does it slip in an assumption it never actually earned?

families.name: Other off-point appeals
families.prompt: Does it pull on something that doesn’t bear on what’s true?

families.name: Other word tricks
families.prompt: Does vague or loaded wording do the real work?
cues: where do you draw the line

ad_hominem.text: Does it argue against the idea, not the person who said it?
Does it judge the claim on its own, not on distrusting the speaker?
Does it answer the original point instead of switching to the speaker?

strawman.text: Does it argue against the claim as actually made, not a stretched version?
Does it restate the other side in a way they'd accept?

tu_quoque.text: Does it answer the criticism, not point at the critic's own conduct?
Is any inconsistency it raises truly the issue, not just a dodge?

appeal_to_authority.text: Is it built on reasons you can check, not mainly who backed it?
Does any cited expert fit the topic and not stand in for the whole case?
Would the case still make sense without the famous name attached?

appeal_to_emotion.text: Is there still a real reason once fear, pity, or anger is set aside?
Would its reasons still stand if the case were put calmly?
Is what pushes you toward the conclusion a reason, not mainly a feeling?

appeal_to_nature.text: Does it judge good or bad by real effects, not by the "natural" label?
Does it check this case for benefit or harm, not just "natural"?
Does it tie its verdict to real benefit or harm, not "natural means good"?

bandwagon.text: Is it built on reasons, not on how many people believe it?
Does it give evidence beyond "most people" or "everyone" agreeing?

hasty_generalization.text: Does how broad the conclusion is match how much evidence backs it?
Are the examples a fair sample, not hand-picked or one-off stories?

false_cause.text: Does it show how one thing actually causes the other, beyond timing?
Does it rule out other explanations like coincidence or a shared cause?
Is the case more than "one came first, so it caused the other"?

slippery_slope.text: Are the steps from the first move to the feared outcome backed up?
Is the predicted outcome the right size for the small step discussed?
Is each link in the chain shown to be likely, not just assumed?

false_dilemma.text: Does it show the real range of options, not just two?
Does it leave a workable middle ground or "do both" on the table?
Does it allow positions beyond just two camps?

circular_reasoning.text: Is the support a separate reason, not a restatement of the conclusion?
Would it persuade someone who doesn't already accept the conclusion?
Does the reason add something the conclusion didn’t already say?

argument_from_incredulity.text: Does it reject the claim on evidence, not because the speaker can’t picture how it’s true?
Does it give a real reason against the claim, not just “I don’t see how”?
Does the doubt point to something unlikely about the claim, not just a gap in imagination?

poisoning_the_well.text: Does it take up the point after it's made, not label the source first?
Does it judge each thing the person says on its own?

genetic_fallacy.text: Does it judge the idea by what it says now, not where it came from?
Does it look at the idea's worth now, no matter how it started?

red_herring.text: Does it stay on the topic being discussed?
Does the point it raises bear on the original question?

whataboutism.text: Does it answer the criticism on its own terms?
Does it deal with the original charge directly?

moving_the_goalposts.text: Does it accept the asked-for evidence as settling the point?
Does it stick to the standard it set up front?

non_sequitur.text: Does the conclusion follow from the reasons given?
Does it spell out the step linking reasons to conclusion?

appeal_to_fear.text: Does it show the scary outcome is likely, not just frightening?
Does it size the danger to what's known, not blow it up?

appeal_to_pity.text: Does it give reasons the point holds, not just sympathy?
Does any hardship it raises really bear on what's being decided?

appeal_to_ridicule.text: Does it give a reason the view is wrong, not just a laugh?
Does it take the view as meant, not as a mocking version?

appeal_to_spite.text: Does it give reasons the point is right, not someone getting what they deserve?
Does any wrongdoing it raises really bear on what's being decided?

appeal_to_tradition.text: Does it give reasons it works, not just that it's old or traditional?
Does it show what the long practice produced, beyond just lasting?

appeal_to_novelty.text: Does it give reasons it works better, not just that it's new?
Does it show a real gain over the old option, beyond being latest?

snob_appeal.text: Does it point to the thing's real qualities, not who fancy picks it?
Does it give reasons the thing is good, not just that it's exclusive?

false_equivalence.text: Does it compare things that really match in scale and seriousness?
Does it limit the comparison to what the two things actually share?

perfect_solution.text: Does it weigh the leftover flaws against the real gains?
Does it judge the option against real choices, not a perfect ideal?

middle_ground.text: Does it back the middle position with its own reasons, not just being in the middle?
Does it let the better-backed side win, even if that side is an extreme?

begging_the_question.text: Does it back up its starting reasons instead of taking the key one for granted?
Does it argue the disputed point openly instead of assuming it settled?

loaded_question.text: Does the question leave the disputed point open instead of baking it in?
Can you answer it straight without first granting an unproven assumption?

appeal_to_ignorance.text: Does it give real evidence for the claim, not just a lack of evidence against it?
Does it keep an unproven claim open instead of calling it false for lack of proof?

shifting_burden_of_proof.text: Does the person making the claim give support, instead of asking others to disprove it?
Does the claim have to be backed up, not just assumed true until disproven?

argument_from_silence.text: Does it build on what the source says, not on something it didn't mention?
Does it show the source would surely mention this before reading meaning into its silence?

anecdotal.text: Does it back the claim with broad evidence, not just one person's story?
Does the wider data carry the conclusion, not one vivid case?

cherry_picking.text: Does it weigh evidence that points the other way, not just the helpful bits?
Does it show the full mix of cases, not only ones that fit the conclusion?

sampling_bias.text: Does it pick a sample that stands for the whole, not just whoever was handy?
Does the sample match the group the conclusion is about?

overgeneralization.text: Does it limit the rule to where it holds, instead of saying it never fails?
Does it allow exceptions instead of forcing the rule onto a case that doesn't fit?

post_hoc.text: Does it give evidence beyond the order of events, not just 'it came after'?
Does it show the sequence repeats, not just one coincidental before-and-after?

single_cause.text: Does it treat the outcome as having several causes, not just one?
Does it weigh other contributing causes instead of ignoring them?

correlation_causation.text: Does it show one thing actually causes the other, beyond them tracking together?
Does it show which way the cause runs, not just that the two move together?

weak_analogy.text: Does it point to shared traits that matter to the conclusion, not just surface looks?
Do the similarities run deep enough to actually carry the conclusion?

false_analogy.text: Do the two cases match where the point depends on it?
Does the parallel hold right where the argument turns, not just elsewhere?

apples_to_oranges.text: Does it measure both by one shared standard that fits each of them?
Are the two alike enough in kind that comparing them means something?

equivocation.text: Does each key word keep one meaning from start to finish?
Does it still work when the key term is pinned to one fixed meaning throughout?

amphiboly.text: Does the key sentence have just one clear way to read it?
Is the key sentence worded so only one meaning is possible?

accent.text: Does it read the statement with plain emphasis, not a tilted one?
Does it still mean the same with the original wording and stress?

definist.text: Does it define the key word without pre-deciding the conclusion?
Does the definition it uses match the word's normal meaning?

composition.text: Does it prove the whole has the trait, not just each part?
Does it show the trait still holds when the parts combine?

division.text: Does it prove each part has the trait, not just the whole?
Does it show the trait reaches each part, not just assume it?

ecological_fallacy.text: Does it keep a group average as a claim about the group?
Does it back a claim about one person with evidence about that person?

affirming_the_consequent.text: Does it allow the result could happen even if the stated condition isn’t true?
Does it check and rule out other routes to the same result first?

denying_the_antecedent.text: Does it allow the result could still happen another way, even with the condition absent?
Does it show the stated condition is the only path before ruling the result out?

base_rate_neglect.text: Does it weigh how common the thing is, not just the test result?
Does it fold in how rare the thing is when judging the odds?

gamblers_fallacy.text: Does it keep each independent try at the same odds?
Does it avoid treating the opposite outcome as overdue after a run?

regression_fallacy.text: Does it notice an extreme case tends to swing back toward normal?
Does it rule out a natural swing back to average first?

survivorship_bias.text: Does it count the cases that failed, not just the ones still around?
Does it check whether the cases that failed shared the same trait before crediting it?

texas_sharpshooter.text: Does it name the pattern in advance, not draw the target around a cluster afterward?
Does it weigh all the cases, not just spotlight one cluster and ignore the misses?

appeal_to_consequences.text: Does it tie the verdict to evidence it’s true, not to how nice believing it would be?
Does it reject a claim only when something shows it false, not because the result is unwelcome?

appeal_to_motive.text: Does it weigh the claim on the evidence, not on what the speaker stands to gain?
Does it treat a hidden motive as a cue to check the point, not proof it’s false?

two_wrongs.text: Does it defend the act on its own, not by pointing at another wrong?
Does it judge the act on its own, treating "others did it too" as no excuse?

ad_hominem_circumstantial.text: Does it address the view itself, not whether the speaker's situation makes it expected?
Does it treat the speaker's situation as a bias to check, not proof they're wrong?

complex_question.text: Does it ask about one thing at a time, not several fused into one demand?
Does it let each part be answered on its own, not force one answer for both?

no_true_scotsman.text: Does it let a counterexample count, instead of relabeling it away?
Does it stick to membership rules set up front, not ones added after the example?

suppressed_correlative.text: Does it keep both sides of the pair meaningful, not stretch one to swallow the other?
Does it keep a real line between the options, not empty one category out?

appeal_to_force.text: Does it rest on reasons the claim is true, not on a threat for disagreeing?
Does it leave you free to disagree, with no penalty for doing so?

appeal_to_flattery.text: Does it persuade with reasons a stranger could weigh, not by flattering you?
Does it still hold once any praise is set aside, because real reasons back it?

appeal_to_wealth.text: Does it show quality by how the thing performs, not by how much it costs?
Does it weigh the claim on its reasons, not on how rich the speaker is?

appeal_to_common_sense.text: Does it give actual reasons, instead of just calling the claim obvious?
Does it answer doubts on the facts, not by calling the doubter foolish?

loaded_language.text: Does it back the judgment with reasons, not just words that pre-decide it?
Does it still hold up when charged words are swapped for plain neutral ones?

weasel_words.text: Does it name a specific source you could look up, not a vague "some say"?
Does it put real evidence behind the claim, not just a softening word?

continuum_fallacy.text: Does it accept the real gap between clear cases, even with no exact cutoff?
Does it accept that many small steps can add up to a real difference?

reification.text: Does it use "society" or "the market" as shorthand, not a thing with a will?
Does it rest on the real people and facts behind the abstraction?

buckets.name: It misses the point
buckets.prompt: The reply seems aimed at something other than the actual point.

buckets.name: It points at the wrong thing
buckets.prompt: It seems to lean on feelings, popularity, or authority instead of reasons.

buckets.name: It assumes too much
buckets.prompt: It seems to take something for granted it should have proven.

buckets.name: It claims more than it shows
buckets.prompt: The conclusion seems to go further than the evidence supports.

buckets.name: It plays on words
buckets.prompt: The wording itself is doing something sneaky.

buckets.name: The steps don’t add up
buckets.prompt: The reasoning steps, or the numbers, seem to go wrong.
